questions.question:
A recent survey shows that 60% of tobacco users were advised to stop consuming tobacco. The survey also shows that 3 out of 10 tobacco users attempted to stop using tobacco.

Based only on the information in the above passage, which one of the following options can be logically inferred with certainty?

A. A majority of tobacco users who were advised to stop consuming tobacco made an attempt to do so.
B. A majority of tobacco users who were advised to stop consuming tobacco did not attempt to do so.
C. Approximately 30% of tobacco users successfully stopped consuming tobacco.
D. Approximately 30% of tobacco users successfully stopped consuming tobacco.
questions.answer: B. A majority of tobacco users who were advised to stop consuming tobacco did not attempt to do so.

understand.plain: The passage gives two statistics: 60% of tobacco users were advised to stop, and 3 out of 10 (30%) attempted to stop. We must infer only what is guaranteed by these two facts together, without adding any outside assumptions. The key insight is that 60% were advised but only 30% attempted, which means at least some who were advised did not attempt. Option B says a majority of those advised did not attempt — this can be logically deduced since 30% attempted out of 60% advised, so at most 30% of the 60% advised actually attempted, meaning at least half of the 60% group did not attempt.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that must be true given the premises, with no room for doubt based solely on what is stated.
keywords.example: If 60% were advised and only 30% attempted, we can be certain that not all who were advised attempted.

keywords.explain: More than half — strictly greater than 50% of a group.
keywords.example: If 60% were advised and at most 30% of all users attempted, then at most 50% of the advised group attempted, so the majority of the advised group did not attempt.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed — this is a pure text logical reasoning question.

given.aim: Identify which statement can be logically inferred with certainty from the two survey statistics.

terms.meaning: Of all tobacco users, 60% received advice to stop.
terms.example: In a group of 100, 60 were advised.
terms.connects: This is the base group for option B's reasoning.

terms.meaning: 30% of all tobacco users attempted to stop (not 30% of those advised).
terms.example: In a group of 100, 30 attempted to stop.
terms.connects: Combined with the 60% advised, at most 30 of the 60 advised could have attempted.

terms.meaning: The conclusion must be true under every scenario consistent with the given data.
terms.example: C and D claim 'successfully stopped' — success is never mentioned in the passage, so these cannot be inferred with certainty.
terms.connects: Eliminates options C and D immediately.

given.plan: Check each option: C and D introduce 'successfully stopped' which is not in the passage — eliminate. A says majority of the advised group attempted: advised = 60%, attempted = 30% total, so at most 30/60 = 50% of advised attempted — NOT a majority. B says majority of advised did not attempt: at least 30 out of 60 did not attempt (50%), and possibly more since some who attempted were not advised — so majority (>=50%) of those advised did not attempt. B is correct.

steps.title: Assign numbers for clarity
steps.formula_raw: P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)
steps.apply: Suppose 100 total tobacco users. Advised = 60. Attempted = 30.
steps.note: These numbers are consistent with the passage — 60% and 3/10 = 30%.

steps.title: Analyse option A
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: A claims majority of the 60 advised made an attempt. At most 30 of the 60 attempted (since only 30 total attempted). 30/60 = 50% — that is NOT a majority (majority requires > 50%). So A is not certain.
steps.note: Even in the best case, exactly half attempted — a majority requires strictly more than half.

steps.title: Analyse option B
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: B claims majority of the 60 advised did NOT attempt. At least 30 of the 60 did not attempt (= 50%), and likely more. Since the passage does not say all 30 who attempted were from the advised group, the number of advised who attempted is at most 30, so at least 30 of 60 = at least 50% did not attempt. This is a majority. B is always true.
steps.note: Option B is the only inference guaranteed by both statistics.

steps.title: Eliminate C and D
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: C and D both mention 'successfully stopped consuming tobacco'. The passage never mentions success rates, only attempts. No inference about success can be made with certainty.
steps.note: Introducing concepts not in the passage violates the certainty requirement.

solution.result: B. A majority of tobacco users who were advised to stop consuming tobacco did not attempt to do so.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is required — the reasoning uses simple arithmetic percentages and the definition of logical inference.

questions.question:
Students of all the departments of a college who have successfully completed the registration process are eligible to vote in the upcoming college elections. However, by the time the due date for registration was over, it was found that surprisingly none of the students from the Department of Human Sciences had completed the registration process.

Based only on the information provided above, which one of the following sets of statement(s) can be logically inferred with certainty?

i. All those students who would not be eligible to vote in the college elections would certainly belong to the Department of Human Sciences.
ii. Students from departments other than Human Sciences failed to complete the registration process within the due time.
iii. All the eligible voters would certainly be students who are not from the Department of Human Sciences.

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (i) and (iii)
C. Only (i)
D. Only (iii)
questions.answer: D. Only (iii) — All the eligible voters would certainly be students who are not from the Department of Human Sciences.

understand.plain: The passage states two things: (1) a student is eligible to vote if and only if they completed registration, and (2) no Human Sciences student completed registration. From (1) and (2) combined, no Human Sciences student is eligible to vote. Statement iii says all eligible voters are NOT from Human Sciences — this is the contrapositive of 'Human Sciences students are not eligible,' which is certainly true. Statement i reverses the direction and claims non-eligible students must be from Human Sciences — this is false because students from other departments could also have failed to register. Statement ii claims students from other departments failed to register — this is not stated anywhere.

keywords.explain: If P then Q is logically equivalent to: if not Q then not P.
keywords.example: If registered then eligible (P->Q), so contrapositive: if not eligible then not registered. That is different from 'if not eligible then Human Sciences.'

keywords.explain: A student who completed registration. The passage makes this the only criterion for eligibility.
keywords.example: A Human Sciences student, having not registered, is by definition not eligible.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 200' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='200' rx='12' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='100' rx='140' ry='70' fill='#E8E6F0' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='160' cy='100' rx='80' ry='45' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='60' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>All Students</text><text x='175' y='95' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Registered</text><text x='175' y='112' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>= Eligible</text><rect x='310' y='70' width='140' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='380' y='95' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Human Sciences</text><text x='380' y='112' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>(no registration)</text><line x1='305' y1='100' x2='250' y2='100' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#D85A30'/></marker></defs><text x='277' y='93' class='sub no' text-anchor='middle'>outside</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing the registered (eligible) set inside all students, with Human Sciences completely outside the registered set.

given.aim: Determine which subset of statements i, ii, iii can be logically inferred with certainty from the passage.

terms.meaning: Completed registration <=> eligible to vote (the passage makes this a biconditional in context).
terms.example: Any registered student is eligible; any eligible student is registered.
terms.connects: Establishes the logical link between registration and voting rights.

terms.meaning: The set {Human Sciences students} and the set {registered students} have empty intersection.
terms.example: If HS student x exists, x is not registered, so x is not eligible.

terms.meaning: Only conclusions that follow necessarily from the given information count; anything that goes beyond the passage is not a certain inference.
terms.example: The passage says nothing about whether non-HS students registered — so statement ii cannot be inferred.
terms.connects: Used to eliminate statements i and ii.

given.plan: Analyse each statement using the two given facts. Statement iii: eligible -> registered -> not Human Sciences. This is a valid chain; iii is certain. Statement i: not eligible -> Human Sciences? Not valid — other dept students could also fail to register. Statement ii: non-HS students failed to register? Not stated — passage only says HS students failed.

steps.title: Formalise the given facts
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Let R(x) = x completed registration, E(x) = x is eligible, HS(x) = x is a Human Sciences student. Fact 1: R(x) -> E(x) (registered implies eligible). Fact 2: forall x, HS(x) -> not R(x) (no HS student registered).
steps.note: Combined: HS(x) -> not R(x) -> not E(x), so all HS students are ineligible.

steps.title: Evaluate statement iii
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: iii claims: E(x) -> not HS(x). From step 1, HS(x) -> not E(x). Contrapositive: E(x) -> not HS(x). This matches iii exactly — CERTAIN.
steps.note: Statement iii is the contrapositive of the chain derived from the two given facts.

steps.title: Evaluate statement i
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)
steps.apply: i claims: not E(x) -> HS(x). We know HS(x) -> not E(x), but the converse (not E(x) -> HS(x)) does not follow. Students from other departments might also be ineligible (if they failed to register for other reasons).
steps.note: This is the fallacy of affirming the consequent. Statement i is NOT certain.

steps.title: Evaluate statement ii
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: ii claims students from other departments failed to register. The passage gives no information about non-HS registration success. This is beyond the given data — NOT certain.
steps.note: Statement ii adds information not present in the passage and cannot be inferred.

questions.formula_note: The solution relies on contrapositive equivalence and the rule against denying the antecedent to evaluate which statements logically follow from the given premises.

questions.question:
Residency is a famous housing complex with many well-established individuals among its residents. A recent survey conducted among the residents of the complex revealed that all of those residents who are well established in their respective fields happen to be academicians. The survey also revealed that most of these academicians are authors of some best-selling books.

Based only on the information provided above, which one of the following statements can be logically inferred with certainty?

A. Some residents of the complex who are well established in their fields are also authors of some best-selling books.
B. All academicians residing in the complex are well established in their fields.
C. Most of best-selling books are residents of the complex who are well established in their fields.
D. Some academicians residing in the complex are well established in their fields.
questions.answer: X. This question was excluded/marked as bonus in the official GATE IN 2023 answer key. The most defensible answer by strict logic is A: since all well-established residents are academicians, and most academicians are best-selling authors, some well-established residents are best-selling authors — but the word 'most' introduces ambiguity about whether 'most' guarantees 'some' overlap with the well-established subset, making the question contestable.

understand.plain: The passage gives two facts: (1) all well-established residents are academicians, and (2) most of these academicians are best-selling authors. We must find what can be inferred with certainty. The word 'most' (meaning more than half) combined with the set-subset relationship is the crux. Since all well-established residents are a subset of academicians, and most academicians are authors, if the well-established group is large enough relative to the academician group, some overlap with the author group is guaranteed. However, 'most' is ambiguous enough that strict logical certainty is disputed, which led to the question being excluded.

keywords.explain: Every member of set A is also a member of set B: the A-set is a subset of the B-set.
keywords.example: All well-established residents are academicians means WE subset of A.

keywords.explain: 'Most X are Y' means more than half of the X-set belong to Y; it does not mean 'all'.
keywords.example: Most academicians are authors: more than half of the academician set wrote best-sellers.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 220' width='480' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='220' rx='12' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='220' cy='110' rx='160' ry='85' fill='#E8F4F8' stroke='#5AABCE' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='195' cy='110' rx='90' ry='55' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='175' cy='110' rx='45' ry='30' fill='#C6EBD8' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='220' y='40' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Academicians (A)</text><text x='200' y='100' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Most are</text><text x='200' y='116' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Authors (Au)</text><text x='175' y='108' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11'>Well-Est</text><text x='175' y='122' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11'>(WE)</text><text x='340' y='110' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Residents</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Nested sets: Residents (outermost), Academicians (inner), Well-Established subset of Academicians (innermost), with 'most' of Academicians also being Authors.

given.aim: Determine which statement can be logically inferred with certainty from the two survey findings.

terms.meaning: WE subset A: every well-established resident is an academician.
terms.example: If 20 residents are well-established, all 20 are academicians.
terms.connects: This links WE to the academician set for the 'most' inference.

terms.meaning: More than 50% of the academician set are best-selling authors.
terms.example: If 50 academicians live there, more than 25 are best-selling authors.
terms.connects: Combined with WE being a subset, this creates potential overlap.

terms.meaning: An inference is certain only if it holds in every scenario consistent with the premises.
terms.example: D says 'some academicians are well-established' — this requires knowing at least one academician IS well-established, which is not stated; academicians could include people not in the WE group.
terms.connects: This is why the question is disputed and was excluded.

given.plan: Evaluate each option: B reverses the 'all WE are A' direction — not valid. C confuses the direction (books are not residents). D claims some academicians are well-established — not stated (the passage only goes from WE to A, not A to WE). A combines both facts: all WE are A, and most A are authors; if there exist well-established residents (implied by 'many' in the passage), some of them are also authors — but this depends on the 'most' overlap. The question is contested, hence excluded.

steps.title: Formalise the premises
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: P1: forall x (WE(x) -> A(x)). P2: Most x in A satisfy Author(x), i.e., |A intersect Au| > |A|/2.
steps.note: The complex has 'many well-established individuals' — implying the WE set is non-empty.

steps.title: Evaluate option D (Some A are WE)
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)
steps.apply: D: exists x (A(x) and WE(x)). From P1, WE subset A, so all WE are in A. If WE is non-empty, there is some x with WE(x) and A(x), making D true. The passage says 'many well-established individuals' implying WE non-empty, so D holds.
steps.note: D is actually supportable if WE is non-empty, which the passage implies but does not assert explicitly.

steps.title: Evaluate option A (Some WE are Authors)
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: From P1, WE subset A. From P2, more than half of A are Authors. Depending on how large WE is relative to A, some WE members may or may not be Authors. This is not certain without knowing the ratio |WE|/|A|.
steps.note: If |WE| + |Au| > |A|, some overlap is guaranteed (inclusion-exclusion); but we cannot verify this from the passage.

steps.title: Official exclusion note
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: The official GATE IN 2023 answer key marks this question as excluded/bonus (answer code X). Multiple options are arguable depending on interpretation of 'most' and whether the WE set is explicitly non-empty. No option is unambiguously certain.
steps.note: When a question is excluded, no marks are deducted or awarded; all candidates receive full credit.

solution.result: X — Question excluded in official GATE IN 2023 answer key. No option can be inferred with absolute certainty given ambiguity in the passage.

questions.formula_note: The solution applies universal and existential quantifier rules to evaluate the four options against the two given premises, exposing the ambiguity that led to exclusion.

questions.question:
The information contained in DNA is used to synthesize proteins that are necessary for the functioning of life. DNA is composed of four nucleotides: Adenine (A), Thymine (T), Cytosine (C), and Guanine (G). The information contained in DNA can then be thought of as a sequence of these four nucleotides. DNA has coding and non-coding regions. Coding regions — where the sequence of these nucleotides are read in groups of three to produce individual amino acids — constitute only about 2% of human DNA. For example, the triplet of nucleotides CCG codes for the amino acid glycine, while the triplet GGA codes for the amino acid proline. Three nucleotides are then assembled to form a protein.

Based only on the information provided above, which one of the following statements can be logically inferred with certainty?

A. Only (i): The majority of human DNA has a function about which we currently have no understanding.
B. Only (ii): The function of human DNA is not fully understood.
C. Both (i) and (ii).
D. Only (iii): The triplet of nucleotides CCG codes for the amino acid glycine.
questions.answer: D. Only (iii): The triplet of nucleotides CCG codes for the amino acid glycine.

understand.plain: The passage describes DNA structure and notes that only 2% of human DNA consists of coding regions. Three candidate conclusions are presented. Only one can be directly and unambiguously extracted from the passage text itself. The triplet CCG coding for glycine is explicitly stated in the passage. The other statements about 'majority having unknown function' or 'function not fully understood' draw conclusions that go beyond what is stated — the passage says non-coding does not produce amino acids, but does not say we have no understanding of non-coding DNA.

keywords.explain: Part of DNA where nucleotide triplets (codons) are read to produce amino acids.
keywords.example: CCG codes for glycine; GGA codes for proline — both are in coding regions.

keywords.explain: 98% of human DNA that does not directly produce amino acids.
keywords.example: The passage states non-coding regions exist but does not say they are not understood.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 180' width='480' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='180' rx='12' class='bg'/><rect x='30' y='60' width='420' height='60' rx='8' fill='#E8E6F0' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='30' y='60' width='8' height='60' rx='4' fill='#1D9E75'/><text x='50' y='85' class='sub ink'>Human DNA (100%)</text><text x='50' y='105' class='mut sub'>Coding: 2%  |  Non-coding: 98%</text><rect x='30' y='140' width='84' height='20' rx='4' class='ok'/><text x='72' y='154' class='sub' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>Coding 2%</text><rect x='32' y='60' width='8' height='60' rx='2' fill='#1D9E75'/><text x='240' y='30' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>CCG -> glycine   GGA -> proline</text><text x='240' y='48' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>(explicitly stated in passage)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar showing human DNA split into 2% coding (green) and 98% non-coding, with CCG and GGA examples labelled as explicitly given in the passage.

given.aim: Identify which statement is directly and unambiguously supported by the passage.

terms.meaning: A fact directly written in the passage — requires no additional reasoning or assumption.
terms.example: 'The triplet of nucleotides CCG codes for the amino acid glycine' is stated word-for-word in the passage.
terms.connects: This is the only kind of statement that can be inferred with certainty without any risk of over-extension.

terms.meaning: The passage says non-coding regions do not produce amino acids, but does NOT say they are non-functional or not understood.
terms.example: Saying 'we have no understanding' goes far beyond 'not used to produce amino acids.'
terms.connects: Eliminates options A and B and C.

terms.meaning: An inference is certain only if it is entailed by the literal content of the passage.
terms.example: D's statement is directly quoted from the passage; statements i and ii go beyond the literal text.
terms.connects: Confirms D as the only certain inference.

given.plan: Check each option against the literal content of the passage. Statement (iii) / option D: 'CCG codes for glycine' — stated verbatim. Certain. Statement (i) / options A and C: 'majority of DNA has function we do not understand' — passage only says 98% is non-coding, not that its function is unknown. Statement (ii) / options B and C: 'function of human DNA is not fully understood' — passage does not say this. Eliminate A, B, C. Answer is D.

steps.title: Identify explicitly stated facts
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: From the passage: (a) DNA has 4 nucleotides A, T, C, G. (b) Coding regions = 2% of human DNA. (c) Triplets in coding regions code for amino acids. (d) CCG codes for glycine. (e) GGA codes for proline.
steps.note: Only facts (a)-(e) are given; any statement outside these cannot be inferred with certainty.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (i): majority of DNA has unknown function
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: The passage says non-coding = 98%. It does NOT say non-coding regions have no known function or unknown function. Non-coding DNA can have regulatory, structural, or other roles not mentioned. Statement (i) cannot be inferred — NOT certain.
steps.note: Going from 'non-coding' to 'no function we understand' is an unsupported logical leap.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (ii): function of DNA not fully understood
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: The passage makes no claim about our level of understanding of DNA. It only describes the coding mechanism. Statement (ii) imports scientific knowledge not in the passage — NOT certain.
steps.note: Inferring 'not fully understood' requires outside knowledge, violating the 'based only on the information provided' constraint.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (iii): CCG codes for glycine
steps.formula_raw: from for-all x F(x) infer F(a) for any constant a
steps.apply: The passage explicitly states: 'the triplet of nucleotides CCG codes for the amino acid glycine.' This is a direct, verbatim fact from the passage. CERTAIN.
steps.note: No reasoning required — this is directly read from the passage, making D the only answer inferred with certainty.

solution.result: D. Only (iii): The triplet of nucleotides CCG codes for the amino acid glycine.

questions.formula_note: The solution requires no special formula — it applies the basic principle that only what is explicitly stated in the passage can be inferred with certainty.
